metadata.summary: Emit structured JSON logs with correlation IDs, mask sensitive fields, use log levels properly, attach context via middleware, prevent PII leaks.

content.title: Structured Logging Patterns
content.summary: Emit structured JSON logs with correlation IDs, mask sensitive fields, use appropriate log levels, attach context via middleware, integrate with aggregators, prevent PII leaks.

why.p: Structured logs are machine-parseable — log aggregators (Datadog, Loki, CloudWatch) can filter and alert on JSON fields without regex. A missing request ID makes distributed debugging nearly impossible. Sensitive data in logs (passwords, tokens, PII) creates compliance and security incidents. Excessive DEBUG logs in hot paths degrade throughput.

list.item: Setting up logging for any new service (Python or TypeScript)
Adding audit trails for compliance (payments, auth events)
Integrating with OpenTelemetry for distributed tracing
Diagnosing production incidents where correlation is required
Short scripts or one-shot jobs — standard print/stderr is fine
Test output — pytest captures output; structured logs add noise
Very high-frequency inner loops — log outside the loop or sample

section.name: logging-format
section.p: Structural rules for log format, levels, sensitive data, and context attachment.

rule.statement: Emit structured JSON logs in production; human-readable format only in development.
rule.rationale: JSON logs are parseable by aggregators (Datadog, Loki, CloudWatch) without regex. Human-readable logs are faster to scan interactively.

rule.statement: Attach correlation ID (request_id) to every log entry via context variable, not per-call argument.
rule.rationale: Per-call arguments are forgotten; a context variable propagates automatically through the call stack once set at request entry.

section.name: log-levels
section.p: Appropriate log levels guide signal-to-noise ratio and alerting thresholds.

rule.statement: Use DEBUG for diagnostic data only active during development; INFO for normal business events; WARNING for recoverable anomalies; ERROR for failures requiring investigation; CRITICAL for system-stopping conditions.
rule.rationale: Treating INFO as the default for everything drowns signal in noise and raises alerting thresholds unnecessarily.

section.name: sensitive-data
section.p: Protecting sensitive information in logs prevents credential leaks and compliance violations.

rule.statement: Never log passwords, tokens, API keys, credit card numbers, SSNs, or full PII. Mask before writing using a structlog processor or Pino redact config.
rule.rationale: Log aggregators are often less secured than the primary database. A leaked log dump becomes a credential dump.

section.name: performance
section.p: Logging overhead in hot paths can dominate execution time.

rule.statement: Do not log inside tight loops or hot paths. Log the aggregate result after the loop completes.
rule.rationale: Each log call serializes a JSON object and may involve I/O. Inside a loop processing 100k items this dominates execution time.

section.name: structlog-setup
section.p: Configuration pattern for the structlog library.

rule.statement: Configure structlog once at application startup with: contextvars merge, log level, ISO timestamp, sensitive data processor, and JSON renderer.
rule.rationale: Processor order matters — merge context before masking sensitive data, render JSON last.

section.reference: Production structlog setup with context vars and sensitive data processor

section.name: request-middleware
section.p: Middleware-based context propagation ensures request IDs reach all log calls.

rule.statement: Set request_id and user_id context variables in middleware at request entry; never pass them as function arguments.
rule.rationale: Middleware runs before any handler; setting context vars here means all downstream log calls automatically include them.

section.name: business-events
section.p: Structured logging of domain-specific state changes creates audit trails and analytics foundations.

rule.statement: Log every significant business state change (order paid, user registered, payment failed) as a structured event with entity_type, entity_id, and relevant domain fields.
rule.rationale: Business event logs serve as an audit trail and analytics source. They must be queryable by entity ID and event type.

example.title: Business event log
example.code: logger.info( "order.paid", entity_type="order", entity_id=order.id, customer_id=order.customer_id, total=float(order.total), items_count=len(order.items), )

section.name: performance-logging
section.p: Threshold-based alerts surface slow operations automatically.

rule.statement: Use a context manager or decorator to log operation duration; emit a WARNING when duration exceeds a configured threshold.
rule.rationale: Threshold-based warnings surface slow operations automatically without requiring manual timing instrumentation everywhere.

section.name: antipatterns
section.p: Common failure modes in logging implementations.

antipattern.description: Log an error then immediately re-throw it (log-and-throw).
antipattern.reason: The same error is logged twice — once at the origin and once at the outer handler — creating duplicate entries that inflate error counts and confuse incident timelines.

antipattern.description: Log passwords, tokens, authorization headers, or credit card numbers without masking.
antipattern.reason: Log aggregators and log shipping pipelines often have weaker access controls than primary storage. A single log export leaks all credentials.

antipattern.description: Mix structured and unstructured log lines in the same service (some JSON, some plain text).
antipattern.reason: Log parsers must handle two formats. A single unstructured line breaks field-level alerting for the entire log stream.

antipattern.description: Log entries with no request ID, user ID, or entity ID (no context).
antipattern.reason: Contextless logs cannot be correlated to a specific request or user. Debugging requires guessing from timestamps alone.

antipattern.description: Build log message strings with f-strings or concatenation before passing to the logger.
antipattern.reason: The string is constructed even when the log level is filtered out. Pass structured key-value pairs — the logger skips serialization entirely for filtered levels.

template.purpose: Production-ready structlog setup with context vars and sensitive data processor
FastAPI request logging middleware with request ID propagation
